root_motivation: Every wintermute daemon already speaks agorabus over a local Unix socket; the fleet vision requires that wm.* events are visible fleet-wide across machines, but NATS has no UDS listener and existing local clients must keep working unchanged — so a bridge sidecar is the only correct topology: it keeps the UDS for locals and opens a NATS TCP connection for the fleet, with a loop guard so bridged events don't echo.
user_persona: Joe Yen running wintermute on an Arch Linux laptop; later on every machine in the constellation fleet. The daemon runs unsupervised as a systemd user unit alongside agorabus.

acceptance_criteria.description: wm-busbridge subscribes to the local agorabus UDS and a local NATS leaf and mirrors an allowlisted wm.fleet.* event published locally onto the matching NATS subject — verified against an embedded/test NATS server via async integration test.

Local UDS clients are unchanged: an existing agorabus subscriber works identically with the bridge running, requiring no code or socket change — asserted by running a mock subscriber alongside the bridge in test.
Loop guard: an event injected from NATS into the local bus is NOT re-published back to NATS — exactly-once delivery proven by counting NATS publishes in a selftest with a mock NATS server.

Selective forwarding: a high-volume/local-only topic (wm.audio.speech.chunk, wm.local.*) is NOT forwarded to NATS; only allowlisted fleet topics cross — asserted by publishing a blocked topic and verifying zero NATS messages received.

The NATS topology config sets a JetStream domain on hub and leaves; a leaf client can reach a hub JetStream stream via the domain-qualified API prefix — documented in config files and asserted in a unit test parsing the NATS config.
Per-node NATS credentials come from an encrypted store path; the repo contains no plaintext creds — grep-asserted: no .creds/.nkey files committed, and the config references a path variable.
wm-busbridge selftest subcommand passes end-to-end (local publish → NATS once → back into a second local subscriber) and the daemon does not panic on a closed pipe (SIGPIPE reset present in main).

scope: Bridge daemon (wm-busbridge) that connects the local agorabus UDS to a local NATS leaf node
Allowlist-based selective forwarding of wm.fleet.* subjects to NATS
Loop guard: tag bridged messages so NATS-originated events are not re-forwarded to NATS
NATS topology config files for hub (JetStream domain=hub, leafnode listener) and leaf (per-machine)
wm-busbridge selftest subcommand for end-to-end round-trip verification
Presence forwarding: node agorabus presence onto a fleet subject
non_goals: Mesh networking / MagicDNS — that is constellation-mesh
Cloud hub host provisioning — that is constellation-cloud
Work dispatch on JetStream — that is constellation-dispatch
Modifying agorabus itself or its protocol
NATS server binary or embedded NATS server in the bridge (only a NATS client)

additional.sigpipe_reset: sigpipe::reset() must be the first statement in main()
additional.async_runtime: tokio with full features for async bridge loop

five_whys_trace.q: Why do you need an agorabus-NATS bridge?
five_whys_trace.a: agorabus only works over a local Unix-domain socket; wintermute daemons on different machines cannot see each other's wm.* events.

five_whys_trace.q: Why can't you just make agorabus speak NATS directly?
five_whys_trace.a: NATS has no Unix-domain-socket listener (confirmed open/unimplemented upstream), so you cannot point NATS at the UDS socket — the two protocols are incompatible at the transport layer.

five_whys_trace.q: Why must existing local clients remain unchanged?
five_whys_trace.a: Every wintermute daemon (wm-stt, wm-brain, wm-audio, etc.) already speaks agorabus over the UDS; changing all of them just to get fleet visibility would be a massive disruptive migration with no benefit to local operation.

five_whys_trace.q: Why is NATS specifically the right cross-host bus rather than MQTT or Redis pub/sub?
five_whys_trace.a: NATS's subject pub/sub maps directly onto wm.* topics and uniquely provides leaf-node topology — each machine is a leaf that serves local consumers first and queues cloudward traffic during disconnects. MQTT/Redis can't match this NAT-friendly architecture, and JetStream gives durable work-queues needed by constellation-dispatch.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Should the bridge connect to a remote NATS hub or a local leaf?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Bridge connects to a local leaf at 127.0.0.1:4222; the leaf dials the remote hub. This decouples the bridge from WAN connectivity.

ambiguities_resolved.question: How to prevent bridged events from looping back to NATS?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Tag bridged messages with a header or subject prefix so an event that arrived from NATS is not re-published to NATS. Each message traverses the bridge at most once per direction.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Which NATS client library to use?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: async-nats (the official async Rust client) to stay consistent with tokio async runtime already used in wintermute.
